title: Significance with zero background and a small upper uncertainty

posts.name: Arash Jofrehei
posts.text:
Dear experts,
I have a simple counting experiment with one background and signal process. The background comes from an ABCD method and since one region has zero events, the nominal value of it is zero. I should note that the other two control regions have very high number of events and hence negligible uncertainty, but I guess we should account for the 1.81 Poisson uncertainty of 0 and propagate it, leading to a background with nominal of 0, lower bound of 0, and a very tiny upper bound of let’s say 0.001.
How can I implement this in Combine? It does not like a background process with 0 events, and does not accept asymmetric uncertainties for zero as far as I know. I did try to set the background to 1e-6 with an upper bound of 1e-3 which I set as a systematic, and there was no run-time error at least when creating the workspace. But I tested it by calculating the significance of 1 observed event and it returns 0, which I guess is because the upper bound of the background is much larger than its nominal and it can fluctuate up.
Best regards,
Arash
posts.cooked_html:
Dear experts,

I have a simple counting experiment with one background and signal process. The background comes from an ABCD method and since one region has zero events, the nominal value of it is zero. I should note that the other two control regions have very high number of events and hence negligible uncertainty, but I guess we should account for the 1.81 Poisson uncertainty of 0 and propagate it, leading to a background with nominal of 0, lower bound of 0, and a very tiny upper bound of let’s say 0.001.
How can I implement this in Combine? It does not like a background process with 0 events, and does not accept asymmetric uncertainties for zero as far as I know. I did try to set the background to 1e-6 with an upper bound of 1e-3 which I set as a systematic, and there was no run-time error at least when creating the workspace. But I tested it by calculating the significance of 1 observed event and it returns 0, which I guess is because the upper bound of the background is much larger than its nominal and it can fluctuate up.

Best regards,
Arash

posts.name: Kyle Cormier
posts.text:
Hi Arash,
Sorry for the delayed response – I think even with 0 observed events for some region, you should be able to implement the standard ABCD method as given here for example in the documentation [1], at least as long as the 0 observed are not in the region diagonal to the signal region.
I didn’t test this explicitly, but I don’t think that having 0 observed will cause any problem as it just means that your likelihood will have a term in it of the form \\mathrm{Poiss}(0;n\_\\mathrm{exp}). If you only have 1 bin per parameter of your ABCD and you have 1 signal, then you’ll have four free parameters and four data points, so the best fit value of n\_\\mathm{exp} will also be 0. But that I don’t think there is anything wrong with it per se, and all the uncertainties should be getting propogated properly, including the Poisson uncertainty on the data statistics.
One situation I see in which it could cause problems is if the region where you have zero observed events is diagonal to your signal region.  But I think this is more a conceptual problem than a combine-related problem. If you think of it as a ‘transfer method’ rather than an ABCD method in that case saying you have a transfer factor estimate with only a lower bound on it (and with best fit value of infinity). So then, I suspect you could run into problems where your fit will want to have an infiite background in your signal bin and a negative infinite signal strength to compensate.
I don’t know if the significance of 0 is correct, but in a situation where e.g. A is your signal region, and you have the equation A = BD/C (i.e. C is diagonal to A) then if you had e.g. D = 0, B = 10e6, C = 10, then I would indeed expect the significance to be very small.  If you think of it as a ‘transfer factor’ D/C which you are measuring and then applying to B to estimate A, you see that given poisson statistics, your transfer factor could easily be 10%, and so Signal region background expectations of 10e5 are entirely reasonable, and observing 1 event is not going to be significant.
If you’d like to share your datacards, I can take a quick look later.
Best,
Kyle
[1] https://cms-analysis.github.io/HiggsAnalysis-CombinedLimit/latest/part2/settinguptheanalysis/?h=abcd#rate-parameters

Hi Arash,

Sorry for the delayed response – I think even with 0 observed events for some region, you should be able to implement the standard ABCD method as given here for example in the documentation [1], at least as long as the 0 observed are not in the region diagonal to the signal region.

I didn’t test this explicitly, but I don’t think that having 0 observed will cause any problem as it just means that your likelihood will have a term in it of the form \\mathrm{Poiss}(0;n\_\\mathrm{exp}). If you only have 1 bin per parameter of your ABCD and you have 1 signal, then you’ll have four free parameters and four data points, so the best fit value of n\_\\mathm{exp} will also be 0. But I think this is more a conceptual problem than a combine-related problem. If you think of it as a ‘transfer method’ rather than an ABCD method in that case saying you have a transfer factor estimate with only a lower bound on it (and with best fit value of infinity). So then, I suspect you could run into problems where your fit will want to have an infiite background in your signal bin and a negative infinite signal strength to compensate.

I don’t know if the significance of 0 is correct, but in a situation where e.g. A is your signal region, and you have the equation A = BD/C (i.e. C is diagonal to A) then if you had e.g. D = 0, B = 10e6, C = 10, then I would indeed expect the significance to be very small. [1] https://cms-analysis.github.io/HiggsAnalysis-CombinedLimit/latest/part2/settinguptheanalysis/?h=abcd#rate-parameters
